nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found in deeper rocky reef areas with rich growths of sponges and algae, generally at depths of 15–40 meters

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on filamentous algae, diatoms, sponges, and tunicates in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept Spirulina, marine algae preparations, high-quality angelfish formula, mysid shrimp, and frozen meaty foods. Generally advised to offer feedings at least three times per day to maintain condition.

summary: A striking Philippine endemic angelfish, generally considered advanced care due to dietary needs and reef-nipping tendencies; tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics.
careNotes:
The Bluespotted Angelfish (Chaetodontoplus caeruleopunctatus) is endemic to the Philippine Archipelago and has been reported at depths of 15–40 meters in rocky reef zones rich in sponges and algae. It is generally considered suitable only for advanced or experienced aquarists due to its dietary complexity and reef incompatibility.

Adult size: Sources vary considerably — FishBase and LiveAquaria report up to 20 cm (8 in), while BlueZoo Aquatics cites 14 cm (5.5 in) and Reeflex cites 12 cm. The averaged working range used here is 12–20 cm; the FishBase scientific maximum of 20 cm total length is considered the most authoritative upper bound.

Tank size: Minimum volume recommendations ranged from 75 gal/284 L (BlueZoo) to 125 gal/473 L (LiveAquaria, Tropical-Fish-Keeping) to 1000 L (Reeflex). The average across sources is approximately 558 L; 473 L (125 gal) has been used as the `minVolumeLiters` value as it represents the consensus of most hobby sources. The 1000 L recommendation (Reeflex) may reflect a more natural/species-appropriate standard and should be considered for larger adult specimens. This field is flagged for reviewer check.

Reef safety: Majority of sources (three of four) classify this species as not reef-safe. It has been known to nip at stony corals, soft corals, and the mantles of tridacnid clams. LiveAquaria listed 'with caution', but most other sources advise against reef tank placement.

Temperament: Generally described as semi-aggressive. Tends to be tolerant of non-angelfish tankmates but has been known to show aggression toward conspecifics and other angelfishes. Best kept singly in most cases.

Diet: In the wild this species is documented feeding primarily on sponges and tunicates in addition to algae. Captive specimens are generally advised to receive varied offerings multiple times per day. Transition to prepared foods can take patience.

Breeding: Chaetodontoplus caeruleopunctatus is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sex changer). Pelagic eggs and pelagic larvae have been documented but no reproducible aquarium breeding protocol has been established to date.
breedingNotes: Documented as a protogynous hermaphrodite; individuals are reported to start as females and dominant individuals transition to male. Spawning produces pelagic eggs and larvae, but successful captive breeding has not been established in the published literature.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (cites 5.5 in / 14 cm, lower than other sources), tank size (75 gal vs 125 gal consensus), reef safety (no vs with-caution). Care level listed as intermediate vs advanced elsewhere.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, max TL 20.0 cm, Philippine endemic, reef-associated habitat at 15–40 m depth, family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cites adult size 12 cm, tank minimum 1000 L (outlier vs 473 L consensus), temperature 22–28°C. Used for temperature cross-check and size averaging.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 8 in / 20 cm, tank 125 gal, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, care level difficult/advanced, semi-aggressive temperament, reef not safe. Also confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism and lifespan 10–15 years.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite. Pelagic eggs and larvae documented in the wild; no established captive breeding protocol reported in the literature.
